action: Read WAI-Guide.md, WAI-State.json, WAI-State.md. Foundation incomplete, 0 sessions, no app code. One open lug: trk-001 complete foundation.
thinking: Standard wakeup protocol. The spoke shell exists but foundation is empty — all identity fields null. Per the guide, no work should start until foundation is complete. Asked the user what Tracks is before doing anything. This was correct behavior but I missed the inbox check — no habit yet of checking lugs/inbox/ on wakeup. The user had to prompt me to look there.

open.item: Check lugs/inbox/ on every wakeup — not yet a habit

focus: Reading inception lug from inbox — trk-inception-v2
action: Read lug_tracks_spoke_bootstrap_2026_03_16.md. Full build spec: origin story, file structure, data model, build order, verify checklist. Tracks is a Next.js progress tracking app.
thinking: The inception lug was a rich, fully-formed build spec. Sparky authored it inside PathFinder, the framework enriched it, and it arrived here as the definitive build order. The lug said 'read this fully before doing anything, present your understanding, wait for Green Light' — I followed that. The spec described a Next.js 14 app, but I flagged immediately that PathFinder was on Next.js 16.1.6 and the constraint said to match PathFinder exactly. That version discrepancy mattered.
insights: Inception lug format: self-contained briefing authored by a prior agent, enriched by framework, delivered to inbox
Check inbox on wakeup — this lug was waiting and I almost missed it

focus: Reading Sparky's artifacts in PathFinder and PathFinder stack
action: Read TRACKS_README.md, TRACKS_SPOKE_COMPLETE.md, BOOTSTRAP_TRACKS_BASE.sh, BOOTSTRAP_TRACKS_BEST.sh, PathFinder package.json, tsconfig.json, layout.tsx, next.config.ts, postcss.config.mjs, globals.css
thinking: The inception lug said to read Sparky's artifacts before writing any code — good discipline. PathFinder's actual stack was meaningfully different from what the spec said: Next.js 16.1.6 not 14, React 19, Tailwind v4 with @tailwindcss/postcss not the old config, no src/ directory. The constraint 'match PathFinder's stack exactly' meant I should follow PathFinder's actual current state, not the stale version numbers in the spec. I also noted PathFinder uses root-level app/ not src/app/ — the inception spec used src/, which I kept since it was explicit.
insights: PathFinder on Next.js 16.1.6 / React 19 / Tailwind v4 — inception spec said 14, match actual not spec
Tailwind v4 uses @import 'tailwindcss' in globals.css and @tailwindcss/postcss — no tailwind.config.ts needed

action: Updated WAI-State.json foundation, closed trk-001, wrote package.json/tsconfig/next.config.ts/postcss/types/storage/all API routes/all pages/README/copied bootstrap scripts, ran npm install, typecheck clean
thinking: Executed the inception spec faithfully. Foundation values applied from the lug, trk-001 closed. The full Next.js app scaffold — types, storage layer with sync fs I/O matching PathFinder's pattern, four API route files, dashboard/new/detail pages with Tailwind styling, README from Sparky's TRACKS_README.md. Typecheck passed clean on first run. The bootstrap scripts were copied from PathFinder as instructed — they belong here not there. Everything looked correct against the spec.
decisions: Match PathFinder's actual stack (Next.js 16) not inception spec version numbers (14)
Use src/ directory structure as explicitly specified in inception lug

focus: Fixing Turbopack error on npm run dev
action: Removed webpack polling config from next.config.ts, replaced with turbopack: {} — error resolved
thinking: Next.js 16 defaults to Turbopack and the webpack polling config from PathFinder triggered a warning. PathFinder added that config for WSL file watching under Next.js 14/webpack. Turbopack handles file watching natively and doesn't need it. The fix is trivially simple: replace the entire webpack config block with turbopack: {}. This is a pattern worth noting — PathFinder configs don't always translate directly to new projects even when matching the same stack version.

fossils.concept: webpack polling config for WSL (poll: 2000, aggregateTimeout: 500)
fossils.reason: Next.js 16 defaults to Turbopack which handles WSL file watching natively — webpack config triggers error

focus: User flags project did not manifest as intended — what is Tracks really?
action: User asked if I understand how WAI generates session files called tracks. Identified that Tracks the project is about WAI session track files, not a generic goal-tracking app.
thinking: This was the critical pivot of the session. The name 'Tracks' was pointing at something specific in the WAI ecosystem — the session record files that WAI generates — not a generic progress tracker. I had built an entire Next.js app solving the wrong problem. My initial guess was correct: Tracks is a viewer/explorer for WAI session track files. But the user's follow-up clarified further — it's not even a viewer. It's a documentation and prompt library that teaches people what track files are and how to generate them. The concept of conversation portability — freeing intellectual product trapped in the LLM chat — is the core insight.
insights: 'Tracks' was a domain-specific term in WAI, not a generic noun — project names in Wheelwright carry semantic weight
The entire Next.js scaffold was solving the wrong problem — a common failure mode when executing a spec without understanding the domain

fossils.concept: Tracks as a Next.js goal/progress tracking web app
fossils.replaced_by: Tracks as a documentation and prompt library for WAI session track files
fossils.reason: Fundamental misunderstanding of the domain — 'Tracks' refers to WAI session record files, not generic progress tracks

evolution: execution → convergence: user redirect forced re-understanding of project identity

focus: Revised plan — Tracks as documentation and prompt library
action: Presented revised plan: no app, pure markdown, three prompt variants, three samples, spec, README as manifesto with portability framing and Wheelwright funnel
thinking: The revised plan came together quickly once the correct mental model was in place. Three generation modes map naturally to an adoption curve: closing-request (no setup, works now) → prep-and-request (prime at start) → active-collection (full integration, what Wheelwright does natively). The funnel structure matters — the repo should pull people toward Wheelwright, not just teach a standalone technique. The samples need to be realistic enough to actually demonstrate portability, not just illustrate the format. User approved the plan with corrections.
decisions: No app code, no build system — pure documentation
Three prompt variants as adoption progression
Samples must be realistic enough to feed to an LLM and continue the project
Wheelwright funnel embedded throughout — not an afterthought

focus: User provides detailed plan spec and corrections
action: Received full spec: README structure, track format spec, all three prompt files verbatim, sample file descriptions, repo creation steps. Three corrections: portability framing, bootstrap scripts don't belong here, MIT license same as framework.
thinking: The detailed spec was comprehensive — verbatim prompt content, exact schema, sample scenarios, file naming convention. Key corrections changed the framing meaningfully. 'Portability frees your intellectual product trapped in the LLM chat and gives it back to you' is stronger than generic re-warming language because it frames the value as ownership and freedom, not just convenience. Bootstrap scripts living in prompts/ is correct — the prompts ARE the bootstrap for a Track session, just as shell scripts bootstrap a Next.js app. The license match to framework signals these projects are a family.
insights: Portability framing = ownership/freedom, not just convenience — meaningfully stronger positioning
Prompts/ is the bootstrap equivalent for this project type — the metaphor is consistent

fossils.replaced_by: 'Frees your intellectual product trapped in the LLM chat and gives it back to you'
fossils.reason: Sessions don't need to be stalled — the value is freedom to move and reclaim ownership, not just resumption

action: Wrote WAI-Spoke/lugs/trk-implementation-v1.md — full build spec capturing all file contents, phases, verify checklist
thinking: The implementation lug captures everything needed to execute or re-execute the build: strip instructions, directory structure, exact file content specs for README and spec, prompt file references, sample scenarios, WAI state updates, verify checklist. Writing the lug before executing the build is good discipline — it makes the intent auditable regardless of whether the execution succeeds. Future agents reading this lug can reconstruct exactly what was intended.

focus: Stripping scaffold and building documentation repo
action: Removed all Next.js files (src/, package.json, node_modules, tsconfig, next.config.ts, postcss.config.mjs, bootstrap scripts). Created prompts/, samples/, spec/. Wrote all files.
thinking: The strip was clean — rm -rf on the identified directories, no hesitation needed since the user understood and approved the direction change. The build executed in dependency order: LICENSE → README → spec → prompts (closing-request, prep-and-request, active-collection) → samples (coding, strategy, learning). The samples required the most care — they needed to be genuinely realistic, not thin demos. Each sample was written to demonstrate a different phase progression and include the full range of conditional fields (fossils, files_out, activity arrays) so readers see the format in action.
activity: Bash: rm -rf src/ package.json node_modules tsconfig.json next.config.ts postcss.config.mjs BOOTSTRAP_TRACKS_BASE.sh BOOTSTRAP_TRACKS_BEST.sh
decisions: Samples written to be genuinely replayable — feed to any LLM and say 'continue this project' should work
Each sample demonstrates different phase progression and field coverage

focus: Cleaned up .next/ artifacts and pushed to GitHub
action: Removed .next/ cache and next-env.d.ts left from dev server run. Initialized git, set remote, committed, pushed to github.com/wheelwright-ai/tracks via SSH.
thinking: The .next/ directory was left from the dev server run earlier in the session — important to clean before pushing. Updated .gitignore to explicitly exclude it for future reference even though there is nothing to build now. Git init, remote add, single commit with all files, push via SSH with the id_ed25519 key. Clean push, 23 files.
activity: Bash: git remote add origin git@github.com:wheelwright-ai/tracks.git
Bash: git add . && git commit -m 'Initial release'
Bash: GIT_SSH_COMMAND='ssh -i ~/.ssh/id_ed25519' git push -u origin main

focus: Closeout — WAI state, session summary, memory
action: Updated WAI-State.json session fields, corrected WAI-State.md identity (overridden by external process), appended session summary to WAI-Session-Summary.jsonl, wrote MEMORY.md, committed and pushed closeout changes.
thinking: The WAI-State.json and WAI-State.md had been modified by an external process (code-puppy-2bfd03) during the session, setting the identity to 'Hub Dashboard' — a different concept. This required correcting both files to reflect what was actually built. The MEMORY.md captures the wakeup checklist correction (check inbox), the portability framing, the project identity, and the git/SSH details needed for future sessions. The session track file was noted as missing after closeout — added as the final step.
activity: Bash: git add && git commit && git push

open.item: Add GitHub repo topics for discoverability
Link from wheelwright.ai and framework README
Add session track recording to wakeup protocol — was missing this session

evolution: execution → crystallization: all artifacts shipped, context preserved for next session

summary: Built wheelwright-ai/tracks from inception lug — corrected wrong project identity mid-session, delivered documentation and prompt library for WAI Track file portability concept, pushed to GitHub.
